original_hypothesis.title: buy() Allows Griefing Whitelisted Users by Filling Their Cap Without Consent
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: ILOPool.buy(raiseAmount, recipient) checks whitelist on `recipient` but transfers RAISE_TOKEN from `msg.sender`. An attacker can call buy(maxCapPerUser, victimAddress) to fill the victim's per-user cap allocation, spending the attacker's own tokens. The victim's cap is fully consumed, preventing them from participating on their own terms. The NFT is minted to the victim but with the attacker's chosen amount and timing.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This breaks user autonomy in ILO participation. Combined with ECON-H003 (cap bypass), the attacker could fill legitimate users' caps while bypassing their own, monopolizing the ILO.

proof_of_signal.pattern: buy(raiseAmount, recipient) checks _isWhitelisted(recipient) but transfers from msg.sender; no consent mechanism for recipient

preconditions: Attacker has sufficient RAISE_TOKEN and has approved the ILOPool

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: msg.sender pays but recipient gets position and cap consumed
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Call buy(maxCapPerUser, victimAddress) to exhaust victim's allocation
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker loses RAISE_TOKEN but victim is denied self-directed ILO participation; combined with cap bypass, attacker monopolizes sale

economic_questions: What is the attacker's net cost of griefing N whitelisted users?
Can the attacker recover value from positions minted to victims (they can't, as NFT is owned by victim)?
Combined with cap bypass (ECON-H003), can the attacker capture the entire ILO?
validation_requirements: Confirm buy() succeeds with msg.sender != recipient
Confirm victim cannot buy again after cap is filled
Estimate gas and capital costs for griefing all whitelisted users

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: maxCapPerUser * N (number of victims) in RAISE_TOKEN (attacker loses this)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: No direct profit; enables monopoly via ECON-H003 combination
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Negative standalone; positive when combined with cap bypass

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Large RAISE_TOKEN balance (equal to hardCap in worst case)
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Early in sale window before victims can buy
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Victims receive positions they didn't request

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Individual whitelisted users whose caps are filled
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Complete loss of ILO participation control; forced into position at attacker's chosen amount

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm msg.sender != recipient works in buy(); victim cannot re-buy

validation_result.impact_summary: An attacker can call buy(maxCapPerUser, victimAddress) to fill any whitelisted user's per-user cap allocation without their consent, permanently preventing the victim from participating in the ILO on their own terms.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The ILOPool.buy() function accepts a `recipient` parameter and validates whitelist status against the recipient, but transfers RAISE_TOKEN from msg.sender. There is no check enforcing msg.sender == recipient nor any consent/delegation mechanism. This allows any caller with sufficient RAISE_TOKEN to fill a whitelisted user's maxCapPerUser allocation. Once the victim's cap is consumed, subsequent buy() calls for that recipient will revert with 'UC' because _position.raiseAmount == maxCapPerUser. The victim receives an NFT position they did not request, at an amount and timing chosen by the attacker. The attacker loses their RAISE_TOKEN (which is locked in the NFT position owned by the victim), but the victim is denied self-directed ILO participation.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The proof_of_signal correctly identifies the pattern in ILOPool.sol lines 96-135. The whitelist check on line 103 validates `recipient`, the cap check on line 117 validates against `recipient`'s position, and the token transfer on line 131 pulls from `msg.sender`. No code path requires msg.sender == recipient.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The function checks whitelist on `recipient` (line 103) and enforces the per-user cap on `recipient`'s position (line 117), but transfers tokens from `msg.sender` (line 131). There is no requirement that msg.sender == recipient, allowing any caller to consume a whitelisted user's allocation without consent.

calling_sequence: 1. Attacker calls buy(saleInfo.maxCapPerUser, victimAddress) with sufficient RAISE_TOKEN approval
4. Hard cap check passes assuming room available (line 106)
6. balanceOf(victimAddress) == 0, so new NFT minted to victim (lines 111-113)
7. Per-user cap check: maxCapPerUser <= maxCapPerUser - 0 passes (line 117)
10. RAISE_TOKEN transferred from msg.sender (attacker) to ILOPool (line 131)
11. Victim's cap is now fully consumed; any future buy() for victim reverts with 'UC'
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The attack path is straightforward: attacker calls buy() with the victim's address as recipient. The whitelist check (line 103) validates the recipient, not msg.sender. The per-user cap (line 117) is checked against the recipient's position. The token transfer (line 131) pulls from msg.sender. After this call, the victim's position has raiseAmount == maxCapPerUser, so any future buy() call with this recipient reverts at line 117 because saleInfo.maxCapPerUser - _position.raiseAmount == 0.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment configuration is relevant to this defect. The vulnerability exists in the core buy() logic regardless of deployment parameters.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack is fully validated: (1) Attacker has RAISE_TOKEN and approves the ILOPool - standard user action. (2) Victim is whitelisted - required precondition that the attacker can verify on-chain via isWhitelisted(). (3) Sale is active - attacker monitors block.timestamp vs saleInfo.start/end. (4) Attacker calls buy(maxCapPerUser, victimAddress). (5) Victim's cap is exhausted. (6) If victim tries to buy later, the UC check reverts. The attack requires the attacker to spend maxCapPerUser worth of RAISE_TOKEN per victim, which they lose (locked in victim's NFT). This is a griefing attack, not a direct profit attack.
validation_result.impact_assessment: MEDIUM severity. Direct impact: whitelisted users can have their ILO allocation forcibly consumed by an attacker. The victim receives a position NFT with vested liquidity, but at the attacker's chosen amount and timing, not their own. The victim cannot subsequently participate. Combined with the per-user cap bypass (NFT transfer trick from INV-ACCESS-001), an attacker could grief all legitimate users while bypassing their own cap to monopolize the ILO. The attacker's cost is maxCapPerUser * N victims in RAISE_TOKEN, which they lose to the victims' positions.
recommended_mitigations: Add a check `require(msg.sender == recipient, 'UNAUTHORIZED')` in buy() to ensure only the recipient themselves can initiate their purchase
Alternatively, implement an explicit delegation mechanism where whitelisted users can authorize specific addresses to buy on their behalf via a mapping and setter function
If third-party purchases are a desired feature, add a separate authorized delegate mapping that recipients must explicitly opt into
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy ILOManager and ILOPool clone with whitelist enabled. 2. Whitelist address V (victim). 3. Fund attacker A with maxCapPerUser of RAISE_TOKEN. 4. A approves ILOPool. 5. A calls buy(maxCapPerUser, V). 6. Verify V has NFT with raiseAmount == maxCapPerUser. 7. V approves ILOPool with RAISE_TOKEN. 8. V calls buy(1, V) and verify it reverts with 'UC'.
uncertainties: The economic impact depends on whether the attacker values denying the victim participation more than the RAISE_TOKEN they lose
If the ILO succeeds and launches, the victim's forced position may still have value (they receive vested liquidity), partially mitigating the harm
additional_testing_needed: Verify that the victim's forced position actually accrues value post-launch (they receive vested liquidity that can be claimed)
Test combined attack with NFT transfer cap bypass to verify an attacker can grief all users AND monopolize the ILO
Quantify the capital cost for griefing N whitelisted users relative to the ILO hardCap
